title: Maids of honour
subtitle: Small Richmond puff pastry tarts with a curd and almond filling
excerpt: Maids of honour are small round puff pastry tarts from Richmond, Surrey, traditionally filled with curd cheese, sugar, almonds, and lemon. They have been made in the town since at least the 16th century and are still sold by a bakery there.
sourceNotes: Traditional English pastry associated with Richmond, Surrey, and often attributed to the court of Henry VIII at Hampton Court. The name 'maids of honour' supposedly refers to the ladies-in-waiting who enjoyed them. The filling varies between recipes but typically involves curd cheese or cream cheese, almonds, eggs, and lemon. The Newens bakery in Kew has sold them since 1850.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled for up to 2 months. Defrost at room temperature and refresh in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day of baking, but will keep in an airtight tin for 2 days.

content.text: Maids of honour have a puff pastry base, not shortcrust. The filling is made from cream cheese or curd cheese mixed with ground almonds, egg, sugar, and lemon zest. It sets firm in the oven and should have a slight golden colour on top. Do not overfill them: the filling rises and any excess will spill over the pastry edge.

items.prepNote: or raspberry jam, for the base

content.text: Line the tins and make the filling

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Cut the puff pastry into 12 rounds using a cutter just slightly larger than the muffin tin cups. Press each round gently into the cups, pushing it into the base and up the sides. If using jam, add a small teaspoon to each case.
Beat the cream cheese and sugar together until smooth. Beat in the egg and yolk. Fold in the ground almonds, lemon zest, and almond extract.
Spoon the filling into the lined tins, filling each case to about two-thirds full. Do not overfill.
Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the pastry is golden and the filling is set and lightly coloured. Leave in the tin for 10 minutes, then transfer to a rack to cool. Eat at room temperature.

items.symptom: The filling spills over the edges
items.cause: Overfilled or pastry did not come up high enough at the sides.
items.fix: Fill to two-thirds only. Make sure the pastry comes up the full sides of each tin cup.

items.cause: Too much jam on the base, or pastry was thick and sat in moisture.
items.fix: Use jam sparingly or skip it. Place the muffin tin on a preheated baking tray to give the base direct heat.

items.cause: Oven too hot, or baked too long.
items.fix: Maids of honour should be set but not dried out. The top should be pale gold, not brown.

content.text: Maids of honour are associated with Richmond in Surrey. The Newens bakery in Kew has been making them to a secret recipe since 1850. The origin story links them to Henry VIII and the maids of honour at Hampton Court, though the evidence is thin. What is clear is that this is a distinctly English small pastry with a longer history than most.
